verses.text: There was a man of Benjamin, whose name was Kish, the son of Abiel, the son of Zeror, the son of Bechorath, the son of Aphiah, a Benjamite, a mighty man of power. He had a son, whose name was Saul, a choice young man, and a goodly: and there was not among the children of Israel a goodlier person than he: from his shoulders and upward he was higher than any of the people. Guidance: Saul's family is named with a four-generation pedigree — respectable lineage. Kish is 'a mighty man of power' (gibbor hayil — the chapter is signaling military potential). Saul himself is introduced by appearance, not character: a head taller than anyone in the kingdom, handsome. The narrator gives us what Israel asked for in chapter 8 — a king to look like the nations. Tall, good-looking, well-connected. The body is described first because the body is what the people had requested. The heart is not described, because in the years ahead, the heart is what will fail.

There was a man of Benjamin, whose name was Kish, the son of Abiel, the son of Zeror, the son of Bechorath, the son of Aphiah, a Benjamite, a mighty man of power. Guidance: Saul's family is named with a four-generation pedigree — respectable lineage. Kish is 'a mighty man of power' (gibbor hayil — the chapter is signaling military potential). Saul himself is introduced by appearance, not character: a head taller than anyone in the kingdom, handsome. The narrator gives us what Israel asked for in chapter 8 — a king to look like the nations. Tall, good-looking, well-connected. The body is described first because the body is what the people had requested. The heart is not described, because in the years ahead, the heart is what will fail.

The asses of Kish Saul's father were lost. And Kish said to Saul his son, Take now one of the servants with thee, and arise, go seek the asses. And he passed through mount Ephraim, and passed through the land of Shalisha, but they found them not: then they passed through the land of Shalim, and there they were not: and he passed through the land of the Benjamites, but they found them not. When they were come to the land of Zuph, Saul said to his servant that was with him, Come, and let us return; lest my father leave caring for the asses, and take thought for us. Guidance: A simple errand — find lost livestock. Three regions, no luck. The future king of Israel is introduced as a young man who could not even keep track of his dad's donkeys. The detail is the narrator's gift to us; we are meant to feel the unlikelihood of this man becoming Israel's first monarch. His worry is also revealing: 'lest my father leave caring for the asses, and take thought for us.' He thinks about his father's concern. There is decency in the young Saul. The chapter will keep showing it before the kingship complicates him.

He said unto him, Behold now, there is in this city a man of God, and he is an honourable man; all that he saith cometh surely to pass: now let us go thither; peradventure he can shew us our way that we should go. Then said Saul to his servant, But, behold, if we go, what shall we bring the man? for the bread is spent in our vessels, and there is not a present to bring to the man of God: what have we? And the servant answered Saul again, and said, Behold, I have here at hand the fourth part of a shekel of silver: that will I give to the man of God, to tell us our way. (Beforetime in Israel, when a man went to inquire of God, thus he spake, Come, and let us go to the seer: for he that is now called a Prophet was beforetime called a Seer.) Then said Saul to his servant, Well said; come, let us go. So they went unto the city where the man of God was. Guidance: The servant is the spiritually alert one. He knows there is a man of God nearby and proposes consulting him. Saul agrees only after the protocol is sorted (a present). The servant has a quarter shekel; that suffices. The narrator pauses to footnote vocabulary: 'seer' was the older word; 'prophet' the newer. Hebrew language and Hebrew theology have evolved by the time this is written down. Note also: Saul does not even know there is a famous prophet at the next town. He will become king of a nation whose spiritual life has been carried by Samuel for forty years, and he does not recognize the name. The chapter's irony is that the king of Israel needs an introduction to Israel's prophet.

As they went up the hill to the city, they found young maidens going out to draw water, and said unto them, Is the seer here? And they answered them, and said, He is; behold, he is before you: make haste now, for he came today to the city; for there is a sacrifice of the people today in the high place: as soon as ye be come into the city, ye shall straightway find him, before he go up to the high place to eat: for the people will not eat until he come, because he doth bless the sacrifice; and afterwards they eat that be bidden. Now therefore get you up; for about this time ye shall find him. Guidance: A choreography of providence in everyday detail. Young women at the well — a Hebrew topos for divine appointment (Rebekah, Rachel, Zipporah, Samaritan woman). They give directions with precise timing: 'about this time.' Samuel has just arrived for a sacrifice on the high place; will be presiding at the meal. Every step of Saul's chance walk has been lining up with a calendar he did not see. The errand to find donkeys has been carrying him toward a coronation he did not seek.

They went up into the city: and when they were come into the city, behold, Samuel came out against them, for to go up to the high place. Now the LORD had told Samuel in his ear a day before Saul came, saying, Tomorrow about this time I will send thee a man out of the land of Benjamin, and thou shalt anoint him to be captain over my people Israel, that he may save my people out of the hand of the Philistines: for I have looked upon my people, because their cry is come unto me. And when Samuel saw Saul, the LORD said unto him, Behold the man whom I spake to thee of! this same shall reign over my people. Guidance: God told Samuel 'in his ear' — a quiet inner word — twenty-four hours before Saul arrived. Samuel walks out of the city gate at the exact moment Saul walks in. The LORD speaks again the moment they meet: 'Behold the man.' The Greek translation of this line will echo down to Pilate's 'Ecce homo' centuries later. Note God's stated reason: 'I have looked upon my people, because their cry is come unto me.' The same verb (look upon, hear cry) used of Egypt in Exod 3. Even when Israel asks for the wrong thing, God still responds to the right cry — the deep cry under the wrong request. He gives them the king they asked for as a partial mercy because He hears the bondage they could not name.

Then Saul drew near to Samuel in the gate, and said, Tell me, I pray thee, where the seer's house is. And Samuel answered Saul, and said, I am the seer: go up before me unto the high place; for ye shall eat with me today, and tomorrow I will let thee go, and will tell thee all that is in thine heart. And as for thine asses that were lost three days ago, set not thy mind on them; for they are found. And on whom is all the desire of Israel? Is it not on thee, and on all thy father's house? And Saul answered and said, Am not I a Benjamite, of the smallest of the tribes of Israel? and my family the least of all the families of the tribe of Benjamin? wherefore then speakest thou so to me? Guidance: Saul asks Samuel for directions to Samuel's house — he has not even recognized the man he is talking to. Samuel's calm reveal: 'I am the seer.' Then a cascade: come to dinner; I will tell you everything; the donkeys are found; Israel's desire is on you. Saul's protest is moving: 'Am not I a Benjamite, of the smallest tribes?' (Benjamin had nearly been wiped out in Judges 20; it really was the smallest tribe.) He is right about his own modest origins. Whether his humility is genuine or formulaic, the chapter records it. The first time we hear the future king speak about himself, he understates. The same man will later become the king who pursues David out of jealousy and refuses to surrender the crown. The early chapter is a portrait of a man who could have become someone different — and what happens between the early protest and the later pride is what 1 Samuel is about.

Samuel took Saul and his servant, and brought them into the parlour, and made them sit in the chiefest place among them that were bidden, which were about thirty persons. And Samuel said unto the cook, Bring the portion which I gave thee, of which I said unto thee, Set it by thee. And the cook took up the shoulder, and that which was upon it, and set it before Saul. And Samuel said, Behold that which is left! set it before thee, and eat: for unto this time hath it been kept for thee since I said, I have invited the people. So Saul did eat with Samuel that day. Guidance: Samuel takes Saul and the servant to the seat of honor — among thirty guests — and brings out a portion already set aside by the seer's foreknowledge. The 'shoulder' was an honor cut, usually reserved for the priest (Lev 7:32). Samuel hands it to Saul. The implicit message: the prophet is conferring priest-like honor on the future king. He knew Saul was coming; the dinner was scheduled for him before he ever walked the donkey trail.

When they were come down from the high place into the city, Samuel communed with Saul upon the top of the house. And they arose early: and it came to pass about the spring of the day, that Samuel called Saul to the top of the house, saying, Up, that I may send thee away. And Saul arose, and they went out both of them, he and Samuel, abroad. And as they were going down to the end of the city, Samuel said to Saul, Bid the servant pass on before us, (and he passed on,) but stand thou still a while, that I may shew thee the word of God. Guidance: After the public dinner, the private session. Samuel and Saul talk on the rooftop — the cool of the evening, the privacy a king-to-be needs for his first secret. They sleep there; at dawn Samuel calls Saul down. They walk to the edge of town. Then the chapter's most carefully staged moment: Samuel sends the servant ahead and says, 'Stand thou still a while, that I may shew thee the word of God.' Anointing requires solitude. Some words from God cannot be spoken in front of a servant; some moments of calling are between God's mouthpiece and one ear only. The chapter ends in the gentle pause before the most consequential drop of oil in Israel's history; chapter 10 will pour it.

And they arose early: and it came to pass about the spring of the day, that Samuel called Saul to the top of the house, saying, Up, that I may send thee away. And Saul arose, and they went out both of them, he and Samuel, abroad. And as they were going down to the end of the city, Samuel said to Saul, Bid the servant pass on before us, (and he passed on,) but stand thou still a while, that I may shew thee the word of God. Guidance: After the public dinner, the private session. Samuel and Saul talk on the rooftop — the cool of the evening, the privacy a king-to-be needs for his first secret. They sleep there; at dawn Samuel calls Saul down. They walk to the edge of town. Then the chapter's most carefully staged moment: Samuel sends the servant ahead and says, 'Stand thou still a while, that I may shew thee the word of God.' Anointing requires solitude. Some words from God cannot be spoken in front of a servant; some moments of calling are between God's mouthpiece and one ear only. The chapter ends in the gentle pause before the most consequential drop of oil in Israel's history; chapter 10 will pour it.

When they were come down from the high place into the city, Samuel communed with Saul upon the top of the house. And they arose early: and it came to pass about the spring of the day, that Samuel called Saul to the top of the house, saying, Up, that I may send thee away.
